elements: single blue stair-shaped prism block (with two vertical steps and one flat top face, all sides colored solid blue)
solid red rectangular cuboid block (right of the blue block, matching proportions and position from the source)
solid green cube block (in front and left, matching the source composition and size ratio)
white background (no visible ground plane or shadowing, matching the plain paper background of the image)
subtle black edge lines outlining all block edges (line thickness and color tuned to match photo accentuation, not cartoon lines)

interactions.teaches: Manually orbit to explore the true 3D form of each object and understand how block heights and relationships are projected into the 2D image.
Clicking any block reveals a popup with its true geometric class (cube, rectangular cuboid, or stair-shaped prism) and a quick property summary.

demo_steps.title: Flat 2D image: appearance only
demo_steps.narration: At first, you see a flat photo of colored shapes—our 'simple world.' All cues to depth and 3D structure are hidden; it's a purely 2D arrangement with no visible height or relation.

demo_steps.title: Reveal true 3D form
demo_steps.narration: Now try orbiting the scene! Rotating the view uncovers the blocks' 3D geometry: the green shape is a true cube, the red is a rectangular box, and the blue block is a single stair-shaped prism—not two boxes stacked. The real spatial layout emerges from what looked flat.

demo_steps.title: Explore object identity
demo_steps.narration: Click any object to highlight it. You'll see a popup confirming its geometric type and proportions. This demonstrates how, beyond color patches, 3D perception lets us infer real-world shapes—even when the 2D image obscures them.

plan.camera_suggestion: Match the original photo closely: eye height just above the table plane, slight angle from the right, so all major faces are visible and the visual crop, perspective, and relative block scales align with the photograph.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original photo's crop, silhouette, label scale, and whitespace exactly
inline_constraints.visible_ui: no filled or translucent panel; only subtle orbit cursor hint if idle, and at most a 1px border highlight on selection
inline_constraints.explainers: shape/info popups appear outside the figure on block click; no fixed legend or caption inside scene
inline_constraints.framing: keep scene boundaries and margins consistent with the source image; do not fill outside the block arrangement
inline_constraints.camera: replicate original angle, FOV, and crop so 3D fits as seamless inline replacement

plan.notes: Construct the stair block as a single prism with matched step heights and depth, not as two stacked cubes. Edge lines should be thin and low-contrast, not black or cartoonish, and precisely match the original image's visual style. The white scene background must have no visible plane or plate; if needed, set the renderer clear color/paper texture to closely mimic the photograph's pure white paper. All proportions/positions of blocks must be measured from the photo for accuracy. Orbit should restrict extreme vertical or zoom-out angles to preserve context.

plan.contextChunk:
a close analysis shows that there is still an important gap to bridge in
order to build reliable and robust systems. Is the field of computer
vision in the 2020s naively optimistic like in the 1960s? It does not
seem to be the case, but only time will tell.

In this chapter we will revisit the simple-world problem we analyzed in
@sec-simplesystem with a hand-crafted approach by
looking for an appropriate high-performing pretrained model and applying
it out-of-the-box to solve our task.

## A Simple Neural Network

When we introduced the simple world in chapter
@sec-simplesystem, our goal was to recover the
three-dimensional (3D) structure of the scene from a single image
(@fig-simplesystem_revisited_img1).

![Image from the simple world.](figures/simplesystem_revisited/img1.jpg){width="40%" #fig-simplesystem_revisited_img1}

We derived an algorithm to recover the 3D scene structure from the first
principles: we started studying the image formation process, we then
reasoned where the information might be preserved, how to build a
representation that makes the information explicit, and finally how to
integrate all the evidence left in the image in order to recover the 3D
information that was lost during the image formation process.

In this chapter, we will instead download a pretrained model that has
been trained to solve the 3D perception problem from single images. The
current state of the art is MiDaS @Ranftl2021, @Ranftl2022. The system
is a neural network trained end-to-end using a diverse set of real
images that had ground truth depth information and also using stereo
images.


[...]

## From 2D Images to 3D

Let's start running the model on the same input image that we used as a
running example in @sec-simplesystem. We just need to apply the neural
network to the input image, obtain the output disparity, and finally
transform the disparity into world coordinates. We do not have to detect
edges or any of the other features that we computed in @sec-simplesystem. Instead, the neural network has
learned to compute whatever features it deems necessary to solve the
task. We will not retrain the network using images from our simple
visual world.

Just as we did in @sec-simplesystem, to show that the algorithm for 3D
interpretation gives reasonable results, we can re-render the inferred
3D structure from different viewpoints.

![3D reconstruction using a pretrained model. The images show multiple view points of the reconstructed 3D scene from @fig-simplesystem_revisited_img1](figures/simplesystem_revisited/views_midas.jpg){width="100%" #fig-views_midas}

It is quite remarkable that it works somewhat well even though this
system has been trained with natural images, which are quite dissimilar
to the simple world we are using here. However, despite the surprising
outputs, it is clear we cannot avoid a number of errors. For instance,
the ground is not recovered as a flat surface; instead it seems to be
concave. The cubes' edges are not straight and the faces are twisted
instead of flat.

We can compare this result with the 3D images recovered using the
algorithm we defined in @sec-simplesystem:

![3D reconstruction using the simple visual system from @sec-simplesystem](figures/simplesystem/views.png){width="100%" #fig-chatgpt_blockworld}

However, while the learned-based model may have lost accuracy in this

elements: blue step-shaped block (geometric stair-shaped prism)
red rectangular cuboid block
green cube block
base ground plane (light grey, no visible grid)
black outline edges on all blocks

interactions.teaches: Directly orbit the scene to manually inspect the 3D shape of each object from any angle, revealing the correspondence between the image and its spatial interpretation.
Click any block to highlight it and pop up a quick summary of its geometric properties (e.g., 'This is the green cube: all edges equal, six faces.').

demo_steps.title: Single 2D image: no depth
demo_steps.narration: Here is the original 2D image from the 'simple world.' At first glance, you see colored regions separated by black edges, but no explicit depth or shape—just a flat photograph.

demo_steps.title: Orbit to reveal 3D shape
demo_steps.narration: Try orbiting the view! As you drag to rotate, you see that the scene actually consists of three blocky 3D shapes. Now true spatial relationships—like which object is closer—become apparent.

demo_steps.title: Identify shapes by clicking
demo_steps.narration: Click on any block. A popup will tell you that the green object is a cube, the blue one is a staircase-shaped prism, and the red one is a rectangular box. This highlights how 3D perception allows us to categorize objects beyond their 2D silhouettes.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Initial camera is slightly above and to the right, matching the original photo's perspective, so all three blocks are clearly visible with the same height and crop as the image.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original image silhouette, size, label scale, and crop
inline_constraints.visible_ui: no filled panel; orbit hint appears only briefly or on hover, click has no permanent panel
inline_constraints.explainers: hover or click triggers popups with shape information
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace exactly
inline_constraints.camera: infer initial angle and zoom to match the photo for a seamless visual replacement

interactionPlan.notes: Edges for all visible faces should be outlined in thin black lines to visually match the source paper models. Blocks must be colored using the same solid hues. Orbit should restrict extreme top/bottom views to prevent unnatural perspectives. When an object is clicked, only its outline is emphasized; popup is in the parent reader, not embedded in the figure.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The blue step block is rendered as two stacked cuboids, not a single connected stair-shaped prism.
The ground plane with a clear shadow is present in the render but not in the original image.
The perspective/camera angle is significantly higher in the render compared to the original closer-to-horizontal photo view.
The relative spacing/proportions between blocks is not matching the original layout.
Block edge lines in the render are thicker and more consistent than the hand-drawn look in the source.
evaluation.notes: Main shapes are present but the blue stair block is two separate cuboids, view is too top-down, and block layout and proportions are off.
action_items: Model the blue block as a true single stair-shaped prism, not as two separate boxes.
Adjust the camera/view to closely match the source photograph's level and crop.
Refine block positions and relative scales to replicate the original composition.
Remove the visible ground plane or match its color to the plain white in the source.
Make edge lines less stark and tune their thickness to match the more subtle appearance in the original.

feedback.rationale: The 'Wrong-Primitives' failure mode and the action item to model the blue block as a true single stair-shaped prism indicate the conceptual decomposition is incorrect and needs to be fixed at the planning stage.
action_items: Model the blue block as a true single stair-shaped prism, not as two separate boxes.
Adjust the camera/view to closely match the source photograph's level and crop.
Refine block positions and relative scales to replicate the original composition.
Remove the visible ground plane or match its color to the plain white in the source.
Make edge lines less stark and tune their thickness to match the more subtle appearance in the original.

attempts.html:
Drag to orbit.
Click a solid.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Labels are present in the generated version but not in the original source image.
The original figure does not have any UI (buttons, toggles, instruction overlay) visible.
Rendered shadows and ground plane differ, the original has very subtle/no shadow and plainer background.
Outline thickness and color are more prominent/heavy in the generated version compared to the thin dark outlines in the original.
Labels overlap the blocks and distract from the visual match.
evaluation.notes: Geometries are very accurate but label overlays, heavy outlines, and excessive UI make the inline screenshot less faithful to the original reference image.
action_items: Hide all UI elements except at most a single small edge button or reset, and move all label overlays off by default for inline screenshot/jpg.
Reduce outline thickness and label size to match the subtler look of the original image; minimize labels to zero for exact photo replacement.

feedback.rationale: The concept and geometries are correct, but UI hallucination, label management, and visual style misalignments point to execution issues that should be addressed by the generator.
action_items: Hide all UI elements except at most a single small edge button or reset, and move all label overlays off by default for inline screenshot/jpg.
Reduce outline thickness and label size to match the subtler look of the original image; minimize labels to zero for exact photo replacement.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The green cube in the generated figure is much closer to the blue shape than in the source image.
Cast shadows are present in the generated figure but not in the reference image.
Luminance and color of faces are more saturated/dark in the rendering than the source figure.
There is a visible 'Reset View' button in the lower left that does not exist in the reference.
The apparent crop and camera angle is slightly shifted, leading to less white space at the bottom and altered perspective.
evaluation.notes: Shapes and topology are accurately represented, but color, composition, and spacing differ, and there is a non-inline UI element.
action_items: Move the green cube further from the blue shape to more closely match the reference spacing.
Remove or hide the Reset View button so that no UI covers the rendered figure inline.
Lighten the colors (especially the blue and green), reduce material shine, and remove shadows for a flatter, textbook look.
Refine the camera angle and crop to better match the original image's layout and whitespace.

feedback.rationale: The concept is accurately implemented (concept_accuracy 5), but visual discrepancies (hallucinated shadows, UI, spacing, colors) and camera issues are all generator-level fixable rather than evidence of a flawed interaction plan.
action_items: Move the green cube further from the blue shape to more closely match the reference spacing.

Refine the camera angle and crop to better match the original image's layout and whitespace.
